content:
The kinetic data reviewed above quantified adhesion loss rates and degradation magnitudes, yet the underlying question of how failure initiates and propagates at the molecular and microstructural level requires separate examination of failure mode transitions and interfacial mechanisms.

Delamination susceptibility under damp heat is not uniform across encapsulant-substrate interfaces. EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:14]. This asymmetric degradation pattern carries practical significance: the glass-encapsulant bond, which must resist both moisture ingress from the module edge and mechanical stress from thermal expansion mismatch, deteriorates faster than the cell-encapsulant bond despite its higher initial adhesion strength. The mechanism likely involves preferential moisture accumulation at the glass surface, where silane coupling agents undergo hydrolytic cleavage more readily than the direct polymer-cell interface. At extended exposure durations, the consequences of this preferential degradation become severe: at 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion falls to a level generally seen after long-term field exposures of more than 15 years [REF:13]. The convergence between accelerated test data and field exposure outcomes [REF:13][REF:14] validates the relevance of laboratory aging protocols while simultaneously demonstrating that glass-interface delamination is the dominant field failure mode.

The chemical degradation pathway for EVA under damp heat proceeds through a distinctive deacetylation mechanism. EVA encapsulant decomposes into acetic acid under damp heat conditions, which reacts with perovskite active layers causing yellow PbI2 formation and associated performance degradation [REF:12]. This decomposition pathway is unique to EVA among the common encapsulant families, as the vinyl acetate monomer units undergo hydrolytic cleavage to release acetic acid—a trigger that initiates a cascade of secondary degradation effects including corrosion of metallic interconnects, pH-driven attack on coupling agents, and catalytic acceleration of further polymer degradation. The severity of EVA deacetylation depends on both moisture availability and temperature, consistent with the Arrhenius kinetic framework established in the preceding section. Material screening data reinforce the importance of polymer composition: PEVA18 showed inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after 2,000 hours of damp heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:9]. The co-occurrence of optical degradation (transmittance loss) and mechanical property changes (stiffening) in PEVA18 [REF:9] indicates that the deacetylation-driven degradation pathway affects both the bulk polymer and the interface simultaneously, though the relative contribution of each damage mode to adhesion loss remains incompletely resolved.

Industry reliability standards provide a quantitative reference point against which failure mode transitions can be assessed. An EVA manufacturer suggests a minimum of 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination, measured by 180-degree peel, to ensure a 20-year lifetime [REF:10]. When this threshold is considered alongside the observation that EVA adhesion at 10,000 hours of accelerated aging matches field exposure levels beyond 15 years [REF:13], the margin between initial lamination strength and end-of-life performance emerges as a critical reliability parameter. Modules that achieve only marginal initial adhesion—near or below the 53 N/cm threshold [REF:10]—may transition from interfacial to cohesive failure modes well before the intended 20-year service life, particularly when the preferential glass-interface degradation mechanism [REF:14] compounds the rate of adhesion loss.

The sensitivity of adhesion retention to substrate and aging method, rather than to adhesive formulation, represents a finding with broad implications for failure mode analysis. Three-way ANOVA demonstrated that substrate (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) significantly affected bond strength, while the adhesive approach did not reach significance (p = 0.84) [REF:8]. This result implies that failure mode transitions—from adhesive to cohesive, or from interfacial debonding to bulk delamination—are governed primarily by the substrate surface chemistry and the environmental stressor profile rather than by the polymer formulation itself. The resistance of resin-substrate bonds to thermal-aging degradation was found to be material dependent [REF:11], and one-step self-etch adhesives obtained the lowest microtensile bond strength results after both aging treatments, with significantly reduced adhesive capacity after thermocycling [REF:11]. Taken together, the ANOVA finding [REF:8] and the material-dependent degradation patterns [REF:11] suggest a two-tier hierarchy: the substrate dictates the locus of failure (interface vs. bulk), while the adhesive formulation modulates the rate of degradation within that failure mode.

Not all adhesive chemistries exhibit equivalent vulnerability to aging-induced failure mode transitions. The experimental non-methacrylate adhesive (EA 5) experienced no changes in bonding performance (p > 0.05) following the entire 1-year duration of biochemical aging [REF:4]. This stability over a full year of biochemical challenge contrasts sharply with the pronounced degradation observed in EVA systems after equivalent or shorter aging durations [REF:13][REF:12], underscoring that the methacrylate ester linkages common to EVA and related systems constitute a vulnerability that alternative chemistries—such as the non-methacrylate framework in EA 5 [REF:4]—may circumvent. The practical implication is that failure mode transitions from adhesive to cohesive failure under damp heat are not inevitable but are chemistry-dependent, opening a design space for encapsulant formulations that resist hydrolytic cleavage at the interface.

| **Material / Condition** | **Adhesion Outcome** | **Failure Mode Implication** | **Source** |
|---|---|---|---|
| EVA at glass interface | Greater degradation than cell interface | Preferential delamination site | [REF:14] |
| EVA at 10,000 h accelerated aging | Matches >15 years field exposure | End-of-life adhesion reached | [REF:13] |
| PEVA18 after 2,000 h DH | Inadequate transmittance, high stiffness | Discouraged as encapsulant | [REF:9] |
| Non-methacrylate EA 5 (1-year biochemical) | No change (p > 0.05) | Resistant to failure mode shift | [REF:4] |
| One-step self-etch adhesives | Lowest MTBS after aging | Most vulnerable to degradation | [REF:11] |

Several critical gaps remain in the mechanistic understanding of failure mode transitions. The deacetylation pathway is well characterized for EVA [REF:12], but equivalent degradation chemistry for polyolefin, thermoplastic polyolefin, and DVS-PEI systems has not been identified or quantified. The ANOVA finding that substrate dominates adhesive formulation [REF:8] was derived from dental adhesive systems, and its transferability to photovoltaic or flexible electronics encapsulant interfaces remains untested. Furthermore, the elastic modulus dependence of delamination susceptibility—identified as a variable of interest for encapsulant design—lacks direct quantitative correlation with failure mode transition thresholds under damp heat conditions.

**Key Findings**

- EVA at the glass interface degrades preferentially over the cell interface, making it the primary delamination site despite higher initial adhesion strength [REF:14], with adhesion at 10,000 hours matching field exposures beyond 15 years [REF:13].
- Substrate type (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) significantly affect bond strength, while adhesive approach does not (p = 0.84), indicating that failure mode is substrate-governed [REF:8].
- EVA deacetylation under damp heat produces acetic acid that drives secondary degradation cascades [REF:12], while PEVA18 develops inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after 2,000 hours [REF:9].
- Non-methacrylate adhesive chemistry (EA 5) maintained stable bonding performance over 1 year of biochemical aging (p > 0.05) [REF:4], demonstrating that failure mode transitions are chemistry-dependent rather than inevitable.
- The 53 N/cm minimum peel strength threshold for 20-year lifetime [REF:10] provides a reference against which end-of-life adhesion values [REF:13] can be assessed.
